claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to understand a bit about your child or children. 

**First question: How old is your child (or children)?**

This is important because:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over typically don't qualify

Please tell me:
1. How many children you're asking about
2. Their age(s)
3. Their name(s) (this helps me keep track if you have multiple children)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! I'm asking about one child - my son Alex. He's 6 years old and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Thank you for that information. Let me continue the assessment for Alex, who is 6 years old.

Since Alex is under 16, let me check the next requirement:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Given that Alex is only 6 years old and lives at home with you, I assume he's not in paid work. Can you confirm that's correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, he's not working at all - he's only 6!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right?**

This would include things like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed in his own name.

Again, given Alex's age (6 years old), I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm this to continue the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you for confirming. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

This is relevant for older children, but again, at 6 years old, Alex wouldn't be in an apprenticeship. Can you confirm he's not in any apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship - he's just 6 years old!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check about **your residency status**. 

**Question: Do you normally live in the UK?**

To qualify for Child Benefit, you must meet UK residency requirements. This can be satisfied in several ways:

- You normally live in the UK, OR
- You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, OR
- You have pre-settled status and meet certain conditions (working, studying, etc.), OR
- You're a Crown servant living abroad

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That satisfies the residency requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are you responsible for Alex?**

You mentioned earlier that Alex lives with you at home in Manchester. This satisfies the responsibility requirement, as you either:
- Live with the child, OR
- Contribute at least the amount of Child Benefit towards their upkeep

Since Alex lives with you, can you confirm that's still the case?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. This could be:
- The child's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else who has claimed for Alex

Are you aware of anyone else who is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: What is Alex's current living situation?**

I need to check if any special circumstances apply. Is Alex:

1. **Living normally at home with you** (which you've already indicated), OR
2. Currently in hospital for an extended period, OR
3. In local authority care, OR
4. In a foster care arrangement?

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living normally at home with you in Manchester. Can you confirm this is the case?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually, Alex has been in local authority care for a couple of months now, but he's still my child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for sharing that important information. I understand this must be a difficult situation. Let me check the specific rules for children in local authority care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. This is an important factor. When a child is in local authority care, there are specific time limits for Child Benefit eligibility.

**Question: You mentioned Alex has been in care for a couple of months. Can you tell me:**

1. **Has Alex been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks?**
   
   **OR**

2. **Does Alex spend at least 24 hours per week at home with you?**

The rules are:
- Child Benefit can continue for **up to 8 weeks** when a child is in care
- **However**, if the child regularly spends **24 or more hours per week** at home with you, you can continue to claim
- You can also claim again if Alex comes home for **2 consecutive nights per week** or **7+ days in a row**

Which situation applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in care for about 7 weeks, so that's within the 8-week limit, right?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct! Since Alex has been in care for about 7 weeks, which is within the 8-week limit, let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important final question: Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance while he's in care?**

This is a critical factor because:
- If the local council **IS paying** for Alex's accommodation or maintenance, you generally **cannot** claim Child Benefit
- If the local council **IS NOT paying** for these costs, you may still be eligible

Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying for his accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## ✅ Good News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!

Based on the information you've provided, you meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for your son Alex.

### Why You're Eligible:

✓ Alex is 6 years old (under 16)  
✓ He's not in paid work  
✓ He's not receiving benefits in his own right  
✓ You normally live in the UK (Manchester)  
✓ You're responsible for Alex  
✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him  
✓ Although Alex is in local authority care, he's been there for only 7 weeks, which is **within the 8-week limit**  
✓ The local council is **not paying** for his accommodation or maintenance

### Important Time Consideration:

Please be aware that Child Benefit can only continue for **up to 8 weeks** while a child is in local authority care (when the council isn't paying). Since Alex has already been in care for 7 weeks, you have approximately **1 week remaining** of eligibility under these circumstances.

After 8 weeks, you would no longer be eligible unless:
- Alex returns home, OR
- Alex spends at least 24 hours per week at home with you

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply as soon as possible** - either online or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - if you or your partner earn over £60,000, you may need to pay some or all of it back through tax
5. Payments are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

**Apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

I hope this helps, and I wish you and Alex all the best during this time.

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 6 years old, which is under 16 - meeting the age requirement. (2) He is not in paid work of 24+ hours per week. (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right. (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements. (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he is the claimant's child. (7) No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Although Alex is in local authority care, he has been there for only 7 weeks, which is within the 8-week limit allowed. (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance, which would otherwise disqualify the claim.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for their 6-year-old son Alex, including the special circumstances provision that allows claims to continue for up to 8 weeks when a child is in local authority care, provided the council is not paying for the child's accommodation or maintenance.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

(6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he is the claimant's child. (7) No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Although Alex is in local authority care, he has been there for only 7 weeks, which is within the 8-week limit allowed. (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance, which would otherwise disqualify the claim.
